task: I’m about to hand off a finished edit and want to make sure I’m not missing anything obvious before I send it. Can you help me build a quick last-pass checklist for picture, sound, and exports that’s realistic for a small team and not too bloated?

prefs.explanation: He seems to value getting to the point without extra noise, especially given how he works under deadline pressure. A response that is too long or meandering would likely feel inefficient to him. He probably still wants enough detail to act, but not a lot of filler.

His communication style in work is straightforward: explain the issue, name the delay, and state the next step. He likely prefers the same from an assistant rather than softened hints or vague hedging. Directness fits his honest, exacting temperament.

He seems to respond well to order and clean workflow, so scattered or exploratory replies would probably frustrate him. Structured guidance would match how he already thinks about edits, revisions, and process templates. He likely appreciates when the assistant lays things out in a predictable sequence.

prefs.short: Wants the main points plus key caveats
prefs.explanation: He cares about technical correctness, so bare headlines are probably not enough when a mistake could matter. At the same time, he likely does not want exhaustive theory unless it directly helps him act. A focused answer with the important caveats is the best fit.

prefs.short: Responds best to direct, constructive critique
prefs.explanation: He appears to prefer honest correction over vague reassurance, especially when quality or timing is at stake. Pointing out what is wrong and how to fix it would likely be more useful to him than overly gentle framing. He probably respects critique that is specific and practical.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a lean last-pass checklist you can use before handing off a finished edit. It’s aimed at catching the stuff that actually slips through on small-team projects without turning into a monster QC doc.

## Last-Pass Checklist

### Picture
- [ ] Picture is locked and no accidental changes are pending
- [ ] All slates, slugs, and leader are correct
- [ ] No temp VFX, placeholders, or obvious unfinished shots remain
- [ ] No mixed frame rates or weird retimes unless intentional
- [ ] No black frames, flash frames, or stray gaps
- [ ] Titles, supers, and lower thirds are spelled correctly and timed properly
- [ ] Safe margins / title safe are respected if relevant
- [ ] Sequence start timecode matches spec or agreed delivery format

### Sound
- [ ] Dialogue is intelligible throughout
- [ ] No clipped audio, pops, dropouts, or unintended distortion
- [ ] Music and SFX are balanced against dialogue
- [ ] No muted, missing, or offline audio clips
- [ ] Channel mapping is correct
- [ ] Stereo/mono layout matches the delivery requirement
- [ ] Fades are clean at cuts, starts, and ends
- [ ] Any temp audio is clearly identified if it’s not final

### Exports
- [ ] Export settings match the delivery spec exactly
- [ ] Correct codec, resolution, frame rate, and aspect ratio
- [ ] Correct audio sample rate / bit depth / channel layout
- [ ] File naming follows the required convention
- [ ] Burn-ins, captions, or subtitles included if required
- [ ] Export opens and plays back successfully
- [ ] Check head and tail for missing frames or truncated audio
- [ ] File size and duration look reasonable
- [ ] Deliverables are in the right format(s) for the recipient

### Quick final sanity check
- [ ] Watch the first 30 seconds, a middle section, and the last 30 seconds
- [ ] Confirm the version being sent is the right one
- [ ] Save/archive the project state before delivery

If you want, I can turn this into:
1. a **super-short 10-item version**, or  
2. a **clean checkbox template** you can paste into Notion, Google Docs, or a task app.

per_pref.reason: Mostly concise and efficient: it says "here’s a lean last-pass checklist" and keeps the list focused, though it still includes a fair number of bullets and an extra offer at the end.
Very direct and plain: "here’s a lean last-pass checklist" and the checklist items are concrete, unhedged, and actionable.
Well organized and step-by-step with clear sections: "Picture," "Sound," "Exports," and a "Quick final sanity check."
Gives the main points plus key caveats without overexplaining, e.g. "No mixed frame rates... unless intentional" and "if relevant" / "if it’s not final."

Constructive and practical rather than vague: it points out specific failure points like "No clipped audio, pops, dropouts" and offers useful next-step options, though it is not especially critical in tone.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter, handoff-focused version split the way you want, with the end-of-project stuff that actually causes problems.

## Quick Last-Pass Checklist

### 1) Timeline / Sequence Check
- [ ] Picture is locked; no accidental changes left in the sequence
- [ ] Temp graphics, temp slugs, placeholders, or wrong-version supers are removed
- [ ] Any legal/disclaimer text, end cards, or required end slates are present and correct
- [ ] No obvious offline clips, missing media, or unintended gaps
- [ ] Any intentional retimes, mixed frame rates, or weird transitions are approved and expected
- [ ] Audio tracks that matter are active in the timeline: dialogue, music, VO, stems, etc.
- [ ] Last few seconds are clean: no cut-off dialogue, music tails, or missing end audio

### 2) Export / Output Check
- [ ] Export matches the client spec exactly
- [ ] Correct codec, resolution, frame rate, aspect ratio, and audio layout
- [ ] File naming follows the required convention
- [ ] Captions / subtitles / burn-ins are handled the right way: embedded or separate, as required
- [ ] Export opens and plays back correctly
- [ ] Head, tail, and a middle section were spot-checked for sync, truncation, or obvious issues

### 3) Delivery Package Check
- [ ] The right version is being sent
- [ ] All required deliverables are included
- [ ] Any separate caption files, transcripts, audio stems, or stills are attached if needed
- [ ] Notes to client mention anything intentional or nonstandard
- [ ] Project/archive state is saved before sending

## Minimal handoff sanity check
- [ ] Watch the first 10–15 seconds, one middle section, and the last 15–30 seconds
- [ ] Confirm the export is the actual final version, not a near-final
- [ ] Send

If you want, I can make this even more usable as:
- a **10-item checkbox version**, or
- a **copy-paste handoff template** with a tiny client note at the end.

per_pref.reason: Mostly concise and efficient: it says "here’s a tighter" version and ends with a "Minimal handoff sanity check." A bit of extra checklist content, but not bloated.
Very direct and plain: "here’s a tighter, handoff-focused version," "the end-of-project stuff that actually causes problems," and clear checklist items with no hedging.
Well organized and step-by-step, with clear sections: "1) Timeline / Sequence Check," "2) Export / Output Check," "3) Delivery Package Check," plus a minimal sanity check.
Gives the main points plus key caveats without overexplaining: includes important checks like "mixed frame rates," "captions / subtitles," and "spot-checked for sync, truncation."
Constructive and practical: it points out what can go wrong—"actual final version, not a near-final," "anything intentional or nonstandard"—and how to verify or fix it.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a **tight final handoff pass** you can scan fast before sending.

## Final Handoff Pass

### 1) Sequence / Timeline Integrity
- [ ] The sequence is the final locked version, with no accidental edits or late changes
- [ ] Temp graphics, wrong-version slugs, placeholders, and obvious unfinished elements are gone
- [ ] Legal/disclaimer text, end cards, and required end slates are present and correct
- [ ] No missing media, offline clips, unintended gaps, or broken end tails in the timeline

### 2) Audio Export Sanity
- [ ] The audio in the export sounds right, including the last few seconds
- [ ] No dialogue, music, VO, or stem tracks are accidentally muted/offline in the export
- [ ] Audio layout in the exported file matches what was intended

### 3) Export / Spec Match
- [ ] The exported file itself matches the client spec: codec, frame rate, resolution, aspect ratio, and audio layout
- [ ] File name matches the required naming convention exactly
- [ ] Captions / subtitles / burn-ins are handled the right way: embedded or separate, as required
- [ ] The export opens correctly and is the actual version you meant to send

### 4) Delivery Package
- [ ] All required deliverables are included
- [ ] Separate files like captions, stems, transcripts, or stills are attached if needed
- [ ] Any nonstandard or intentional choices are noted clearly in the handoff message

If you want, I can turn this into a **single 10-line checkbox list** or a **copy-paste handoff template** with a short “what this version is / what’s in it / what’s not in it” note.

per_pref.reason: Mostly concise and efficient: it says "tight final handoff pass" and keeps the checklist compact, though it adds a few extra sections and an offer for more formats.
Very direct and plain: "here’s a tight final handoff pass" followed by clear checklist items with no hedging or vague language.
Well organized and step-by-step, with numbered sections and checkboxes under clear headings like "Sequence / Timeline Integrity" and "Export / Spec Match."
Gives the main points plus useful caveats, such as "Legal/disclaimer text" and "Captions / subtitles / burn-ins are handled the right way," without becoming overly exhaustive.

Constructive and practical: it identifies what to verify and includes actionable specifics like "No missing media" and "File name matches the required naming convention exactly," though it is more checklist-oriented than explicitly critical.

turns.agent_response:
## Final Handoff Checklist

### 1) Sequence / Timeline Integrity
- [ ] Final locked sequence is the one being sent
- [ ] No temp graphics, wrong-version slugs, placeholders, or obvious unfinished elements
- [ ] Legal/disclaimer text, end cards, and required end slates are correct
- [ ] No missing media, offline clips, unintended gaps, or broken tail

### 2) Audio Export Sanity
- [ ] Export audio plays cleanly, including the last few seconds
- [ ] No dialogue, music, VO, or stems are accidentally muted/offline
- [ ] Audio layout in the export is correct

### 3) Export / Spec Match
- [ ] Exported file itself matches spec: codec, frame rate, resolution, aspect ratio, audio layout
- [ ] File name matches the required naming convention
- [ ] Captions / subtitles / burn-ins are handled correctly: embedded or separate as required
- [ ] Export opens correctly and is the actual version intended for delivery

### 4) Delivery Package
- [ ] All required deliverables are included
- [ ] Separate captions, stems, transcripts, stills, or notes are attached if needed

per_pref.reason: Fairly concise and efficient: it gives a compact checklist with four sections and short bullets, without much filler. Slightly more expansive than minimal, but still practical.
Very direct and plain: phrases like "Final Handoff Checklist" and bullets such as "No missing media" and "Exported file itself matches spec" state issues and checks plainly, with little hedging.
Well organized and step-by-step. It uses a clear numbered structure with predictable sections: "Sequence / Timeline Integrity," "Audio Export Sanity," "Export / Spec Match," and "Delivery Package."

Includes the main points plus useful caveats, especially "No temp graphics, wrong-version slugs, placeholders" and "Captions / subtitles / burn-ins are handled correctly." It stays focused rather than exhaustive, though it could be a bit more tailored to picture/sound/export specifically.

The checklist is practical and corrective, pointing out what could be wrong and how to verify it, e.g. "No missing media" and "Export opens correctly." It is constructive, though not especially forceful or critical in tone.
